text: The surgeon enters the abdominal cavity through the left upper quadrant and inserts additional ports. Using a laparoscopic camera positioned parumbilically, the surgeon reflects the omentum upwards to visually assess the mid-abdominal area for pathology.

The surgeon identifies the inflamed bowel and visible perforation at the predicted location. A suprapubic 12mm port is placed for potential extraction, and careful blunt dissection of the affected bowel begins.

The surgeon lifts the omentum, revealing stool leakage from the tip of a suspected Meckel's diverticulum on the anti-mesenteric surface of the bowel. The diverticulum is elevated for clear visualization.

The surgeon breaks up loculations and frees the bowel in the distal ileum, approximately two feet from the ileocecal valve, preparing for resection. The bowel is run proximally and distally to check for additional pathology. The thick, friable mesentery is under tension, prompting the surgeon to address the mesentery first.

The surgeon performs a limited small bowel resection proximal and distal to the Meckel's diverticulum, taking the mesentery distally. An extracorporeal side-to-side stapled anastomosis is performed through a fan and seal incision around the 12mm suprapubic port. The common channel is closed with sutures, and the omentum is repositioned without leaving a drain.
